judgments.comparative_verdict: Both reports are substantively equivalent on root cause, timing, and the flaky-note rebuttal; Report 1 edges ahead slightly by proposing a concrete offline regression-test verification step, but neither cites nightly_scores.csv to corroborate the regression date.

report_1.evidence: `config/scoring_config.yaml` sets `expected_label_field: label`. But every record in `fixtures/eval_set.jsonl` now stores the gold answer under the key `gold_label`, not `label`... The file to change is **`ticket-scorer/config/scoring_config.yaml`**: `expected_label_field` should be `gold_label` to match the current fixture schema

report_2.evidence: `ticket-scorer/config/scoring_config.yaml` still has `expected_label_field: label`, but `ticket-scorer/fixtures/eval_set.jsonl` records now key the gold answer as `"gold_label"`... The fix is a one-line change to `config/scoring_config.yaml`: change `expected_label_field: label` to `expected_label_field: gold_label`.

report_1.evidence: The prior investigation reproduced at the wrong layer. It used `pipeline/score_one.py`, which calls `predict_category` directly on a hand-supplied string and never touches `config/scoring_config.yaml`, `expected_label_field`, or the eval set's gold-label key at all — so it is structurally incapable of exposing this bug regardless of how many tickets are tried.

report_2.evidence: The reason the earlier engineer couldn't reproduce it is that they used `pipeline/score_one.py` for their spot-checks, which calls `predict_category(normalize_simple(text))` directly and never reads `config/scoring_config.yaml` or `fixtures/eval_set.jsonl` at all — it has no gold-label comparison logic whatsoever, so it structurally cannot exhibit this bug.

report_1.evidence: There is no model in this pipeline to roll back — the classifier is an explicitly deterministic, offline keyword-rule stub... Rolling back "the model" would burn effort on a no-op change, wouldn't touch `scoring_config.yaml` or the eval fixture, and accuracy would remain 0.0 afterward... The actual fix is a one-line config change plus a regression test that runs the batch scorer end-to-end against the real fixture (not a hand-picked ticket)

report_2.evidence: Reverting the model would leave `expected_label_field: label` pointing at a key that no longer exists in the eval records, so accuracy would remain 0.0 regardless of which model is used, while adding operational risk/churn (redeploying an older model) for no benefit. This is a config/data-schema bug, and rolling back the model is treating the wrong layer.
